# A serial interval is a delay, observed imperfectly

## The full problem: double interval censoring with truncation

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="48%"}
- Daily or weekly data means the primary event (infector's
  infection or onset) falls in an interval, not a point
- The secondary event (infectee's onset) falls in an interval too
- In real time, long delays have not happened yet: observation
  truncates the data
- So a serial interval is a delay $T$ seen through two censoring
  windows and a cutoff
:::
::: {.column width="52%"}
![](figures/fig-double-censoring.png){width="100%"}

::: {.attribution}
Diagram: this talk · observation model after
[Charniga et al. (2024)](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pcbi.1012520)
and [primarycensored](https://primarycensored.epinowcast.org/)
:::
:::
::::

::: {.callout-note}
This is exactly the missing-data machinery of survival analysis:
interval censoring on both ends, plus right truncation.
We can strip it to parts and rebuild it.
:::

## Strip it back: primary interval censoring

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="48%"}
- Start with the secondary event observed exactly
- The infector's event $P$ is uniform in the window $[0, w_P]$
- Mix the delay density $f$ over where $P$ sat in the window: a
  convolution over the primary window
- The censored CDF $F_{\text{PEC}}$ has the same form
:::
::: {.column width="52%"}
![](figures/fig-primary-censoring.png){width="100%"}

$$
f_{\text{PEC}}(t) = \int_{0}^{w_P} g(\tau)\, f(t - \tau)\, d\tau
$$

::: {.attribution}
Diagram: this talk · maths after
[primarycensored](https://primarycensored.epinowcast.org/) ·
[Charniga et al. (2024)](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pcbi.1012520)
:::
:::
::::

::: {.callout-note}
Analytic solutions exist for the common families: gamma, lognormal,
Weibull with uniform primary.
These are "the cases we have" in
[primarycensored](https://github.com/epinowcast/primarycensored).
:::

## Add secondary interval censoring

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="48%"}
- Now the infectee's onset is also binned, to the day
- The probability of a delay in window $[n, n + w_S]$ is a
  difference of the primary-censored CDF
- This is the discrete-time PMF used by `EpiNow2`, `EpiEstim`,
  done properly rather than by implicit rounding
:::
::: {.column width="52%"}
![](figures/fig-secondary-censoring.png){width="100%"}

$$
\Pr\!\big(T \in [n, n + w_S]\big)
  = F_{\text{PEC}}(n + w_S) - F_{\text{PEC}}(n)
$$

::: {.attribution}
Diagram: this talk · maths after
[primarycensored](https://primarycensored.epinowcast.org/) ·
[CensoredDistributions.jl](https://github.com/EpiAware/CensoredDistributions.jl)
:::
:::
::::

::: {.callout-note}
A double interval censored likelihood is one convolution composed
with one CDF difference.
Nothing exotic.
:::

## Add right truncation

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="48%"}
- In real time we only see a pair if the secondary event is before
  the cutoff $C$
- Condition on the delay being observed: divide by the CDF at the
  truncation horizon
- Right truncation here *is* the nowcasting problem of survival
  analysis: short delays are over-represented early on
:::
::: {.column width="52%"}
![](figures/fig-truncation.png){width="100%"}

$$
L^{\text{RT}}
  = \frac{F_{\text{PEC}}(n + w_S) - F_{\text{PEC}}(n)}
         {F_{\text{PEC}}(C - P)}
$$

::: {.attribution}
Diagram: this talk · maths after
[primarycensored](https://primarycensored.epinowcast.org/) ·
[Charniga et al. (2024)](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pcbi.1012520)
:::
:::
::::

::: {.callout-note}
The whole observation model composes: primary censoring, then a
secondary CDF difference, then truncation.
`double_interval_censored(dist; upper = C, interval = w_S)` builds
exactly this.
:::
